skill.name: clinic-sample-size
skill.description: Unified tool for calculating sample sizes for Diagnostic, Efficacy, Etiology, and Prognosis clinical studies. Supports various statistical methods (Sensitivity/Specificity, Log-rank, Chi-square, EPV, etc.).
skill.summary: Calculate sample sizes for diagnostic, efficacy, etiology, and prognosis clinical studies with automatic parameter inference.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 91 static analysis findings are false positives. The 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' findings (51 HIGH) incorrectly flag statistical constants (z-scores like 1.96, 0.84162) and mathematical functions (math.log) used in clinical sample size formulas as cryptographic operations. 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings (36 MEDIUM) flag Markdown code block documentation examples that are not executed code. 'System reconnaissance' findings (4 LOW) flag file path references in an audit results JSON file. 'Hardcoded URL' findings (2 LOW) flag a GitHub link to the skill's own source repository. The filesystem operations (creating output directories and writing Markdown reports) are legitimate for a report-generating tool. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, or malicious patterns were detected.

low_findings.title: Statistical constants misidentified as cryptographic algorithms
low_findings.description: 51 HIGH findings flagged z-score constants (1.96, 0.84162, 1.95996) and math.log calls in statistical sample size formulas as weak cryptographic algorithms. These are legitimate statistical parameters for clinical sample size calculations, not cryptographic operations. The z-scores are standard normal distribution quantiles used in power analysis and hypothesis testing formulas across all calculator classes (DiagnosticCalculator, EfficacyCalculator, EtiologyCalculator, PrognosisCalculator).
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged lines contain either mathematical constants for sample size formulas or text strings in documentation/descriptions. No actual cryptographic operations exist in the codebase.

low_findings.title: Markdown code blocks misidentified as shell execution
low_findings.description: 36 MEDIUM findings flagged backtick-formatted text in SKILL.md as shell command execution. All findings are inside Markdown code blocks (```bash ... ```) that serve as documentation examples showing how to run the Python scripts. These are not executed commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every flagged line is within a Markdown fenced code block used for documentation. The skill documentation shows example CLI usage, not executing commands.

low_findings.title: File path references misidentified as system reconnaissance
low_findings.description: 4 LOW findings flagged references to file paths in clinic-sample-size_audit_result_v2.json and validation logic in calculators.py as system reconnaissance. These are JSON audit metadata fields and input validation for a statistical function parameter check.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The audit result JSON contains skill evaluation metadata with standard field names. The calculators.py line is a hazard ratio input validation check. Neither constitutes system reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: GitHub source URL misidentified as network risk
low_findings.description: 2 LOW findings flagged the GitHub URL in SKILL.md line 7 as a hardcoded URL. This URL points to the skill's own source repository and serves as attribution metadata, not as a network call or data exfiltration endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is a Markdown-formatted link to the skill's public GitHub repository. The skill makes no network requests at runtime. This is standard attribution.

content.user_title: Calculate Clinical Study Sample Sizes Instantly
content.value_statement: Clinical researchers struggle with complex sample size calculations across different study types. This skill automatically computes sample sizes for diagnostic, efficacy, etiology, and prognosis studies with smart parameter inference.
actual_capabilities: Calculate sample size for diagnostic studies using sensitivity/specificity, AUC, Kappa, and EPV methods
Calculate sample size for efficacy trials including two-arm, single-arm, and survival analysis designs
Calculate sample size for etiology studies with categorical and survival analysis modes
Calculate sample size for prognosis studies using EPV, log-rank, chi-square, and t-test methods
Automatically infer missing statistical parameters based on study type and design
Generate detailed Markdown reports with input parameters and calculation results
limitations: Requires basic understanding of clinical study design and statistical concepts
Automatic parameter inference uses conservative defaults that may not fit all research scenarios
Does not support Bayesian or adaptive trial designs
Reports are generated in Chinese language only

use_cases.title: Diagnostic Test Sample Size Planning
use_cases.description: A researcher planning a diagnostic accuracy study uses the skill to calculate required sample size based on expected sensitivity and specificity values.
use_cases.target_user: Clinical researcher designing a diagnostic study

use_cases.title: Clinical Trial Sample Size Estimation
use_cases.description: A biostatistician designing an RCT uses the skill to determine the number of participants needed to detect a specified treatment effect.
use_cases.target_user: Biostatistician planning a randomized controlled trial

use_cases.title: Prognostic Factor Study Design
use_cases.description: A medical researcher designing a prognosis study uses the skill to calculate sample size for a prediction model with a given number of variables.
use_cases.target_user: Medical researcher conducting a prognostic factor analysis

prompt_templates.title: Basic Diagnostic Sample Size
prompt_templates.prompt: Calculate sample size for a diagnostic study with sensitivity 0.85 and specificity 0.90.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick estimate for a diagnostic test evaluation

prompt_templates.title: Two-Arm Efficacy Trial
prompt_templates.prompt: Calculate sample size for a two-arm efficacy study with experimental group mean 0.5, control group mean 0, standard deviation 1 for both groups, and dropout rate 0.1.
prompt_templates.scenario: Planning an RCT with continuous outcome variable

prompt_templates.title: Survival Analysis Sample Size
prompt_templates.prompt: Calculate sample size for a two-arm survival efficacy study with accrual time 12 months, total study time 24 months, control group median survival 12 months, and experimental group median survival 18 months.
prompt_templates.scenario: Designing a clinical trial with time-to-event endpoint

prompt_templates.title: Prognosis Prediction Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Calculate sample size for a prognosis prediction model using EPV method with 10 predictor variables, event rate 0.15, training rate 0.7, and dropout rate 0.1.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building a clinical prediction model with limited events

output_examples.input: Calculate sample size for diagnostic study with sensitivity 0.85, specificity 0.90, error margin 0.05, prevalence 0.3, dropout 0.1.
output_examples.output: The calculator produces a Markdown report with study type, input parameters table, calculation results including required cases based on sensitivity, required controls based on specificity, total raw sample size, final sample size accounting for dropout, and estimated group sizes.

output_examples.input: Calculate sample size for a two-arm efficacy study with medium effect size and default parameters.
output_examples.output: The calculator generates a Markdown report showing continuous variable comparison results including sample size per group, total sample size excluding dropouts, final total sample size, and a Chinese-language conclusion summarizing the findings.

best_practices: Provide specific statistical parameters such as sensitivity and effect size for more accurate calculations instead of relying on automatic inference.
Always specify the dropout rate based on your study population to ensure the final sample size accounts for expected attrition.
Review the inferred parameters in the output report to confirm they match your research assumptions before finalizing the study design.
anti_patterns: Providing contradictory parameters such as setting both continuous and categorical values for the same study type can produce misleading results.
Relying on default inferred values without verifying they are appropriate for the specific disease population and research context.
Using the tool without understanding the underlying statistical assumptions can lead to incorrect sample size estimates.

faq.question: What types of clinical studies does this skill support?
faq.answer: This skill supports four major clinical study types: diagnostic, efficacy, etiology, and prognosis studies.

faq.question: Can this skill calculate sample size without all parameters?
faq.answer: Yes, the skill infers reasonable default values for missing parameters based on study type and design.

faq.question: What statistical methods are available for sample size calculation?
faq.answer: Supported methods include sensitivity/specificity, AUC, Kappa, EPV, log-rank, chi-square, and t-test.

faq.question: In what format are the calculation results saved?
faq.answer: Results are saved as Markdown files with input parameters, calculation results, and conclusions.

faq.question: Can I specify a custom output directory for the reports?
faq.answer: Yes, you can use the output_dir flag to specify a custom directory for the generated Markdown reports.

faq.question: Does the skill support non-inferiority trial designs?
faq.answer: Yes, the two-arm efficacy calculator supports non-inferiority tests when a margin parameter is specified.
